query: retention options for orthodontic midline diastemas

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Spacers can be used before or during braces treatment to increase space interproximally. Spacers are normally utilized between molars if there is not enough space for metal bands to be placed around them. Spacers can be made of different materials based on how long they need to be placed in the mouth. Rubber spacers are traditionally used for 1 to 2 weeks and normally fall out as soon as space is adequate. Metal spacers are commonly used for more than six weeks but can also be used for short-term space management. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Posterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: NiTi Expanders: These nickel-titanium wire shapes can be attached to a lingual sheath welded to molar bands cemented to the maxillary first permanent molars. Various sizes are available and must be selected depending on the amount of expansion desired and the pretreatment width of the palate.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Clear aligner treatment is ideal for patients who cannot have many in-person appointments or are concerned with aesthetics. Unfortunately, there are drawbacks to clear aligner treatment, including patient compliance and the severity of the case. Patients are instructed to wear clear aligners for at least 22 hours daily for an optimum time for the teeth to straighten. Additionally, if malocclusion cases are too severe, clear aligners are not recommended, and traditional braces are usually suggested. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There are several methods to treat malocclusion. Palatal expanders are traditionally used before braces treatment to treat a narrow palate, crowding, or crossbite. Rapid palatal expansion (RPE) works by placing a force on the two maxillary bones to split the mid-palatal suture. Palatal expanders are most useful during adolescence, as the maxillary bones become fused during puberty. The expander contains a screw turned daily or as directed by the patient's orthodontist. Every turn places pressure on the maxillary bone, allowing for less than 1 mm of expansion. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Over the past few years, clear aligners have become increasingly popular, especially in adult patients. Clear aligners utilize CAD/CAM technology to digitally scan the dental arches to create clear removable trays. A clear aligner treatment has many different sets of aligners, each for a different stage of treatment. Patients wear each stage tray for 1-2 weeks, depending on the treatment plan created by the orthodontist.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Anterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: The type of movement required for correction is assessed. Removable appliances can be considered for tipping movement; the fixed appliance is indicated for bodily tooth movement. The appliance should incorporate these features and good anterior retention to counteract the displaying effect of the active element. A bite plane or an active component could move the teeth to free the occlusion with the opposing arch. Fixed appliances can be indicated when there is insufficient overbite to retain the corrected incisors. Open coil springs can be used in straight wire mechanics to create enough arch length to position the teeth. A negative root torque is sometimes required for palatally placed upper incisors. An adequate overbite and a normal inclination of the long axis of the tooth to be treated are important for retention stability.

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of malocclusion. Describe the etiology behind malocclusion. Identify the different classes of malocclusion. Apply best practices when treating malocclusion. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Posterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: The rapid maxillary expansion involves a hyrax screw type of appliance capable of splitting the mid-palatine suture and bringing about skeletal changes in a short interval. The RME screw can be incorporated into 2 types of appliances, the banded RME and bonded RME.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Indications -- Orthodontic Extractions
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth are often considered for extraction for orthodontic reasons, often to create space in the dental arch to allow movement of the teeth into a more favorable position. [21] A plan for which teeth are to be removed should be formulated by an orthodontist.

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Osteotomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: LeFort I osteotomy is used to reposition the dentition-bearing maxilla and is indicated for the following conditions: Maxillomandibular deformities Maxillary hypoplasia or mandibular hyperplasia Vertical maxillary excess Angle’s class II and class III malocclusions Midline discrepancies or asymmetries Apertognathia or open bite Absolute transverse arch discrepancy* Dual or multiplanar occlusion* Severe maxillary atrophy, in conjunction with bone grafting Obstructive sleep apnea Access for skull base tumors Facilitation of reduction of nonreducible fractures [6] [7] [8] *May require segmental or multipiece LeFort osteotomy, which is not covered in this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Posterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: The coffin spring is an omega-shaped wire appliance that corrects crossbite in young, developing dentition. The expansion produced is slow and bilaterally symmetrical.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Orthodontics seeks to achieve esthetic and functional improvement via mechanical therapy that moves teeth into a more ideal position. Determining the ideal dental position for each patient depends on several factors, such as the facial profile, facial balance, and aesthetic concerns. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: While various methods have been proven to treat malocclusion successfully, newer technologies are still being tested to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of treatment. Perhaps in the near future, the utilization of low-level laser therapy on orthodontic movement, for example, will be a common practice in orthodontic offices. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of patients with cleft lip deformity is a long-term commitment. Medical treatment will largely focus on requirements from any concomitant congenital abnormalities and based on nutritional needs. Within the first few weeks to months of life, NAM can be employed with assistance from an orthodontist. This involves the creation of an orthodontic appliance that molds a protruding premaxillary segment and alveolar process into a more favorable position. This allows for repositioning of the alveolar segments, medialization of the alar base, and columellar lengthening, which allows for easier surgical repair of cleft lip and nasal cleft deformity down the line. These require frequent adjustments by the orthodontist. Other treatment adjuncts that assist with decreasing the severity/width of the cleft early on are lip taping (often performed in patients with less severe clefts) and lip adhesion (an approximation of the cleft lip edges without changing lip landmarks or disturbing tissue required for definitive closure, often used in patients with wide clefts who are poor NAM candidates for social or geographic reasons).

retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

This relationship highlights that malposition and malpresentation are considered part of the broader set of indications guiding clinical management during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

Finally, Mentum presentation of fetus is identified as an isa relationship with مّجيء وَجْهي (face presentation), specifying that a mentum (chin) presentation is a particular form of facial fetal presentation. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate how various fetal positions, malpresentations, and physiological reproductive events are organized and interpreted to guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 994
retrieved_docs.content: The fetal presentation entities “Face OR brow presentation of fetus” and “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” are each linked to more generic descriptors—“Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” respectively—indicating that the specific presentations are sub‑categories of broader, episode‑agnostic classifications. Both of these broader presentation categories are classified under the overarching obstetric condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which groups together abnormal fetal positions that deviate from the normal cephalic presentation. “Malposition; malpresentation” itself is identified as an indication for obstetric management within the domain “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” highlighting that any malpresentation warrants specific clinical attention during prenatal care, labor, and delivery planning. Within the “Malposition; malpresentation” group, two additional sub‑types are recognized: “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The prolapsed‑arm condition also has a parent term expressed in Arabic, “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” which serves as the linguistic equivalent of the English descriptor. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates that specific fetal malpresentations—face or brow presentation, transverse or oblique presentation, and prolapsed fetal arm—are organized under the general category of malposition/malpresentation, which is a key indication for specialized obstetric care throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This structured classification aids clinicians in recognizing, documenting, and managing abnormal fetal positions to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 614
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is organized under the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” which in turn is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation. This malposition; malpresentation category is a central classification within “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” indicating that fetal malpositions are important considerations in obstetric management. Malposition; malpresentation also classifies other specific fetal position abnormalities, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” has the parent term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” the Arabic expression for a prolapsed fetal arm, while “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is linked to its parent “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Additionally, the relationship “has parent” connects the concept of malposition; malpresentation with the Arabic phrase “عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين,” which translates to “instability of fetal position,” reinforcing that these malpresentations represent unstable fetal orientations. Collectively, these entities and their hierarchical relationships delineate how various fetal presentations—face or brow, prolapsed arm, transverse or oblique—are categorized under the overarching malposition; malpresentation framework, highlighting their relevance for clinical decision‑making during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.
